text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic procedure using a four-port technique, with a supraumbilical camera and an epigastric liver retraction port. The patient is positioned semi-right lateral to access the upper pole splenic cyst, and the initial attempt involves entering the gastro-splenic ligament to assess the spleen.

An additional five-millimeter port is placed for liver retraction, allowing the surgeon to mobilize the splenic cyst. The gastrosplenic ligament is divided, exposing the splenic cyst in the upper pole.

The surgeon identifies the whitish structure of the cyst located in the posterior and upper pole of the spleen. The upper pole splenic vessels are visible entering the spleen, which is largely occupied by the cyst. Hemlock clip applicators are used to secure the upper polar vessels.

The surgeon continues the mobilization of the splenic cyst from the gastro-splenic ligament, identifying and dissecting the splenic artery and vein at the splenic hilum. The pancreatic tail is visible and carefully dissected away as the procedure advances towards the upper pole of the spleen.

Due to the bulk of the spleen, the surgeon performs posterior dissection with difficulty. A 5mm trocar is inserted directly into the splenic cyst, followed by a suction catheter to aspirate the clear fluid, causing the cyst wall to collapse.

The collapsed upper polar cyst is held by an assistant using a grasper, allowing for easier mobilization. The surgeon dissects the lienorenal ligaments to separate the upper pole of the spleen from the retroperitoneum and diaphragm. As the dissection proceeds cranially, the splenoprenic ligament is divided, facilitating complete mobilization of the splenic cyst.
The surgeon divides the posterior layer of the lienorenal ligament, detaching the topmost attachments of the upper pole of the spleen from surrounding structures.

The surgeon divides flimsy adhesions between the diaphragm and spleen superiorly before commencing the upper pole partial splenectomy. Posteriorly, the posterior layer of the linoleum ligament is divided, and the cyst cavity is entered. A ligature device is used for hemostasis as the splenic parenchyma becomes visible.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to perform an upper pole partial splenectomy, excising approximately eighty percent of the cyst. A small portion of the cyst cavity extending into the middle portion of the spleen is marsupialized and left intact.

The surgeon completes the excision of the cyst along with the upper pole of the spleen. The excised specimen, including the upper pole of the spleen and the cyst, is placed into an endo bag for removal from the surgical field.

The lower pole of the spleen is visibly preserved. The surgeon removes the splenic cyst along with the upper pole through a peri-umbilical incision. A drain is placed into the splenic fossa, completing the procedure without complications.
